era_context: The Late Bronze Age is in its mature, stable phase. The Hittite Empire under Mursili II is consolidating territorial control. Egypt under Ramesses II is in the early-to-middle decades of his reign, a period of military expansion and cultural flourishing. Mycenaean Greece is in the Late Helladic IIIA period. The Shang Dynasty in China is in a period of relative stability. This is an era characterized by palace-centered polities, long-distance trade networks, and military competition between major powers for control of the eastern Mediterranean and Levant.

events.title: Mursili II consolidates Hittite control in Anatolia
events.description: Mursili II continued his policy of consolidating Hittite control over Anatolia and neighboring regions. His reign was marked by military campaigns against rebellious vassal states and rival powers. The Hittite annals record numerous campaigns and the subjugation of territories. Mursili II also maintained diplomatic relations with neighboring powers, including Egypt and Assyria, through a combination of military strength and diplomatic negotiation.
events.certainty_note: Mursili II's reign and general activities are well-attested. The specific year is assigned by Hittite chronology, which has ±10–15 year uncertainty.

events.title: Egyptian-Nubian relations and resource extraction
events.description: During Ramesses II's reign, Egypt maintained tight control over Nubia, extracting gold, ivory, ebony, and other valuable resources. Egyptian administrative records and temple inscriptions document the organization of trade and tribute from Nubian territories. The construction of temples at Abu Simbel, which began during this period, was partly funded by wealth extracted from Nubia. This economic relationship was maintained through a combination of military force and administrative control.
events.certainty_note: Egyptian control over Nubia and resource extraction are well-documented. Dating is secure to within ±2–3 years.

disconfirming_evidence: No disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the extent of Egyptian control over Nubia and the nature of Egyptian-Nubian relations (whether primarily exploitative or involving more reciprocal trade) remain subjects of scholarly debate. Some scholars emphasize the agency of Nubian populations and the complexity of the relationship beyond simple domination.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1819 BCE is heavily weighted toward the Near East and Egypt. Hittite cuneiform records and Egyptian inscriptions provide detailed documentation of political and military events, but are elite-focused and often propagandistic in nature. The record is almost entirely silent on the lives of ordinary people, agricultural conditions, and most of the world's population. The absolute chronology of this period depends on synchronisms between different regional chronologies and carries ±10–15 year uncertainty.

graph_edges.note: Mursili II and Ramesses II were contemporaries; their empires competed for influence in the Levant and maintained diplomatic relations.
